coredata.dc:title: DNA binding graphene quantum dots inhibit dual topoisomerases for cancer chemotherapy
coredata.dc:creator: Geng, Bijiang
Hu, Jinyan
Li, Ping
Pan, Dengyu
Shen, Longxiang
coredata.dc:description: DNA-protein interfacial inhibition is a classical paradigm for design of diverse interfacial inhibitors as clinically relevant anticancer drugs. To extend this paradigm from small molecules to nanoparticles, we synthesized pyridine N-modified polycationic graphene quantum dots (Graphenin) as a new class of DNA probes and interfacial inhibitors for cancer chemotherapy. Compared with classical interfacial inhibitors, Graphenin showed enhanced interfacial inhibition against Topoisomerase I and II, both of which are clinically important targets for chemotherapy. The enhanced interfacial inhibition activity was ascribed to their distinct DNA targeting capability preferentially at the DNA major groove, through which many DNA binding proteins recognize DNA. They induced DNA damage and cell apoptosis for sensitive and resistant cancer cell lines, and great potential for tumor chemotherapy was demonstrated in vitro and in vivo. Our results have identified shape-complementary Graphenin as the first DNA major groove binder to inhibit DNA binding enzymes for chemotherapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
DNA major groove
Topoisomerase I and II
Interfacial inhibition
Anticancer drugs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Topoisomerase I and II inhibition assay

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22108168 , 11774216 , 12175142 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2020M681267 ), and the cultivation project ( ynms202103 ) from Shanghai Jiao Tong University affiliated Sixth People's Hospital.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation and characterization of Graphenin. (a) Structural model. (b) Synthetic procedure. (c) Zeta potentials of solutions at varied concentrations (pH 7.4). (d, e) TEM and high-resolution TEM images. Insets: lateral size distribution and FFT pattern. (f) AFM image. (g) DLS size distribution in PBS and serum. (h–k) Survey XPS spectrum (h), high-resolution C 1s (i), N 1s (j), and O 1s (k) spectra. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Graphenin as a DNA probe selectively binds to MAG. (a) Optical spectra of Graphenin's ethanol solutions. (b) Enhanced PL in Graphenin solution with dsDNA titration. (c) Minor-groove binding assay for Graphenin using Hoechst 33258 as positive control. (d) MAG binding assay for Graphenin using MG as the positive control. The change of the CD spectra of ct-DNA with MG was monitored after addition of different concentrations of Graphenin. (e) DNA agarose gel electrophoresis imaging of Graphenin (small-molecule DNA probes as positive controls). (f) Confocal images of Hela cells co-stained with Graphenin and nuclear probe SYTO 17 excited at 488 nm and 633 nn, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Topoisomerase inhibition by Graphenin. (a, c) Topo-mediated DNA relaxation assays used for test of Graphenin activity against Topo I and II. (b, d) Models of Graphenin induced interfacial trapping of DNA/Topo-I and DNA/Topo-II complexes. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

DNA damage and apoptosis induced by Graphenin. (a) Comet assay tested DNA damage in Hela cells incubated with Graphenin for 24 h. (b) The change in the expression levels of γ-H2AX with varied Graphenin concentration by western blots. Lamin A is used as a loading control. (c) Cell apoptosis and necrosis analysis by flow cytometer. (d) Cell cycle analysis of Hela cells treated by Graphenin. (e) Change in the expression levels of Bax, Bcl-2 and p53 with varied Graphenin concentration. (f) The change in the expression levels of cleaved caspase-3 (Cl-Caspase-3) and caspase-8 with varied Graphenin concentration with or without 50 μM of the pan-caspase inhibitor zVAD-FMK. GAPDH is used as a loading control. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Anticancer activity of Graphenin. (a) Activity of graphenin against tumor cell lines indicated by MTT assay. (b) IC50 indicated. (c) Activity of graphenin against MCF-7 and MCF-7/ADR, HCT-8 and HCT-8/PTX, Hela and Hela/DDP. (d, e) Enhanced suppression of solid tumor growth by locally demonstrated Graphenin using MCF-7 and Hela as tumor models on BALB/c nude mice. (f) Representative photographs of excised tumors from MCF-7 tumor-bearing mice after different treatments. (g) Monitoring the intratumor concentration change of locally injected drugs (Graphenin and DOX) in solid tumors by in vivo imaging. MCF-7 bearing nude mice were imaged using the fluorescence signals from the two drugs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Design of polycationic GQDs (Graphenin) as the first MAG binding nanomedicine to confer pharmacological activities by enhanced interfacial inhibition of Topo-I and Topo-II enzymes.

head.ce:title: DNA binding graphene quantum dots inhibit dual topoisomerases for cancer chemotherapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai, 200444, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai, China 200444

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Shanghai University, Shanghai, 200444, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Sciences, Shanghai University, Shanghai, China 200444

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Shanghai Jiao Tong University affiliated Sixth People's Hospital, Shanghai, 200233, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Jiao Tong University affiliated Sixth People's Hospital

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Shanghai Jiao Tong University affiliated Sixth People’s Hospital, Shanghai, China 200233

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: DNA-protein interfacial inhibition is a classical paradigm for design of diverse interfacial inhibitors as clinically relevant anticancer drugs. To extend this paradigm from small molecules to nanoparticles, we synthesized pyridine N-modified polycationic graphene quantum dots (Graphenin) as a new class of DNA probes and interfacial inhibitors for cancer chemotherapy. Compared with classical interfacial inhibitors, Graphenin showed enhanced interfacial inhibition against Topoisomerase I and II, both of which are clinically important targets for chemotherapy. The enhanced interfacial inhibition activity was ascribed to their distinct DNA targeting capability preferentially at the DNA major groove, through which many DNA binding proteins recognize DNA. They induced DNA damage and cell apoptosis for sensitive and resistant cancer cell lines, and great potential for tumor chemotherapy was demonstrated in vitro and in vivo. Our results have identified shape-complementary Graphenin as the first DNA major groove binder to inhibit DNA binding enzymes for chemotherapy.

Overall scheme to show the design of polycationic GQDs (Graphenin) as the first MAG binding nanomedicine to confer pharmacological activities by enhanced interfacial inhibition of Topo-I and Topo-II enzymes.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: A classical paradigm for developing clinically relevant interfacial inhibitors is based on interfacial trapping of protein-DNA transient complexes [1–6], typically using DNA intercalators as inhibitors through intercalation of an aromatic chromophore moiety between the base pairs. Topoisomerases (Topos) such as Topo-I and Topo-II are two important drug targets that are essential in changing the topology of mammalian cell DNA by generation of transient single-strand breaks (SSBs) and double-strand breaks (DSBs), respectively. Camptothecin derivatives of irinotecan (CPT-11) and topotecan are only FDA-approved single Topo-I inhibitors, while doxorubicin (DOX), daunorubicin, mitoxantrone, and etoposide (VP-16) are typical single Topo-II inhibitors. Indeed, the conventional Topo inhibitors offer effective combination treatment for diverse kinds of cancer, but they are limited by single target mutations [7,8] and multidrug resistance (MDR) [9]. It is thus desirable to develop a new class of dual-target Topo inhibitors with distinct mechanisms of action that could circumvent single-target mutation resistance and MDR [10].

In double-strand DNA (dsDNA), its characteristic major and minor grooves are two molecular recognition targets. Especially, the major groove (MAG) target is more attractive, because most DNA binding proteins perform biological functions by selectively binding to the MAG site [2,11–14]. Theoretically, MAG binders could concurrently interfere with multiple proteins such as Topo-I and Topo-II, if sufficient affinity could be achieved. Unfortunately, MAG binding is usually noted with proteins and peptides [2], which are difficult to overcome biological barriers for action on the nuclear DNA target in cancer cells. Few non-peptidyl small-molecule compounds have been found to show binding preference to MAG with an exception such as methyl green (MG) [12]. Natural products such as pluramycins, aflatoxins, and azinomycins were reported to bind to the MAG, but they primarily act as intercalators [11]. A lack of shape-complementary MAG binders with sufficient affinity to compete with MAG-recognized proteins has limited evaluation of the druggability of the MAG target. A major challenge is the size mismatch between small molecules (far smaller than 1 nm) and the MAG target (1.2 nm width for common DNA-B conformation) [14].

In nanomedicine fields, the interactions between DNA and nanoparticles (NPs) have been extensively studied for bioimaging, biosensing, gene therapy, and drug delivery [15–25]. However, studies on the strong DNA-NP interactions are largely limited to ssDNA rather than dsDNA. Recently, several NPs such as black phosphorus [26,27], fullerene derivatives [28], and Au NPs [29] have been reported to exhibit intrinsic anticancer activity, but little is explored for design and discovery of intrinsically active drugs based on the DNA-NP interactions and their induced biological effects. Among various NPs, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) or derivatives could become candidates for MAG identification to replace biodegradable proteins and peptides, because they could penetrate through multiple biological barriers owing to their distinct, tunable surface properties [21,25,30]. They have an atom-thin planar shape, ultrasmall size (<10 nm), molecular weights comparable to those of peptide compounds, and a large specific surface area exposed to the targeted biological interface [31,32]. Their surface charge and amphiphilic nature can be facilely modified by various charged functional groups [25,33,34]. Particularly, GQDs possess MAG-matching edges, allowing for partial insertion into the MAG via one sharp edge with shape-complementarity. As a superior fluorescent probe [33,35], GQDs also offer a simple tool for monitoring the GQD-DNA interactions without additional labeling.

Based on GQD's distinct shapes and surface properties, we report the discovery of a shape-complementary MAG binder from GQDs. We developed a molecular-fusion strategy for controllable, scalable synthesis of membrane-penetrable, polycationic GQDs as a superior MAG binder. Importantly, the MAG binder can penetrate through multiple intracellular barriers, localize at the nucleus, preferentially bind to the MAG sites of nuclear DNA in cancer cells, and block DNA–protein interactions to elicit unusual antitumor activities like interfacial inhibitors, as shown in Scheme 1 . The interfacial biological effects of MAG-targeted GQDs on Topo inhibition, DNA damage, and cell apoptosis were investigated. Their anticancer potential as a new class of Topo inhibitors were further demonstrated in vitro and in vivo. Our studies provided the first NP formula for development of shape-complementary MAG binders as novel anticancer agents.

Graphenin was synthesized via a one-step molecular-fusion (MF) strategy in organic solvents using an aromatic molecule, julolidine (C12H15N), as the precursor. Typically, 0.1 g julolidine (98%) was dissolved in 30 mL ethanol with addition of 1 mL acetic acid, and then the solution was transferred into a poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave. After heated at 200 °C in an oven for 6 h and cooled down to room temperature naturally, a crude product containing colloidal Graphenin in ethanol was derived. The product was purified by evaporating the solvents in a rotary evaporator. Petroleum ether was then added to selectively dissolve residual julolidine by sonication for 30 min, followed by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 10 min) to remove the impurity. After repeating the purification for several times, a pure product (almost no detectable fluorescence from residual aromatic molecules) was finally obtained in a yield of ∼60%. The yield of Graphenin was determined by weighting the mass of Graphenin before and after purification. The product was stored as colloidal solutions in water or common organic solvents for characterization and biomedical use.

Aberration-corrected Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Atomic force microscope (AFM) observations were taken using a JEOL ARM 200F electron microscope and SPM-9600 AFM, respectively. X ray diffraction (XRD) patterns, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of dried samples were recorded with a Rigaku 18 KW D/max-2550, a Bio-Rad FTS16, and a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD XPS, respectively. A Renishaw in plus laser Raman spectrometer was utilized to measure the Raman spectra. The hydrodynamic diameter and Zeta potential of Graphenin were measured using a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 analyzer (Malvern). The Hitachi 3100 and 7000 spectrophotometers were used to measure the absorption and fluorescence spectra of Graphenin.

Typically, aqueous solutions of Graphenin probe (5 μL, 1 mg mL−1) were added to melted agarose gels (1%) in 1 × TAE buffer (Tris-acetate-EDTA) (30 mL) when cooled to 50–60 °C. Then, gels were electrophoresed in 1 × TAE buffer supplemented with DL2000 Plus DNA Marker (Takara Bio, USA) at 120 V for 0.5 h. After running, gels were imaged in a ChemiDoc MP Imaging System (BIO-RAD, USA). Classical DNA probes with different binding modes were used as positive contrasts: DNA intercalators, propidium iodide (PI) and SYTO 17; a minor groove binder, Hoechst; and a major groove binder, methyl green (MG).

The influence of Graphenin on the interactions between dsDNA and MG was carried out by circular dichroism (CD) spectra (J-1500, JASCO, Japan). MG was purchased from Sigma without further purification before use. Dry purified nitrogen (99.99%) was used to deoxygenate the optical chamber of CD spectrometer for 5 min. CD spectra of ct-DNA (30 μg mL−1) in the presence of MG (25 μM) were initially measured at 25 °C. Then, Graphene with varied concentrations was added to the mixture and incubated for 20 min. Finally, the CD spectra of the mixture were performed to investigate the influence of Graphenin on the interactions between DNA and MG.

MCF-7 human breast cancer cells, Hela human cervical cancer cells, 4T1 mouse breast cancer cells, Hgc-27 human gastric carcinoma cells, HepG-2 human hepatocellular liver carcinoma cell line, MG-63 osteogenic sarcoma cell line, PANC-1 human pancreatic carcinoma cell line, HCT-8 human colon carcinoma cell line, A375 malignant melanoma cell line, and A549 human lung cancer cells, were originally obtained from Chinese Academy of Sciences. The culture medium of MCF-7, Hela, HepG2, MG-63, PANC-1, A375, and A549 was DMEM. The culture medium of Hgc-27, 4T1, and HCT-8 was RPMI 1640.

Cellular uptake of Graphenin was quantified by flow cytometry. Briefly, Hela cells were seeded on 24-well plates (5 × 104 cells/well) and treatment with Graphenin for 2 h at 37 °C. Then, the fluorescence intensity was measured by flow cytometer. To elucidate the detailed uptake mechanism, Hela cells were initially treated with Cytochalasin B (50 μg mL−1), Dynasore (40 mM), or Chlorpromazine (10 μg mL−1) for 30 min. Besides, Hela cells treated with Graphenin for 2 h at 4 °C and room temperature (r.t.) were also performed. After incubating, Hela cells were washed and the fluorescent intensity was quantified by flow cytometry.

Approximately 2 × 105 Hela cells were seeded in culture dishes (35 mm dish with 14 mm bottom well) and incubated for at least 24 h. To monitor the rapid dynamical processes of Graphenin internalized into cells at room temperature (25 °C), Hela cells were exposed to aqueous solution of Graphenin. After removed extracellular Graphenin, cells were immediately imaged under a laser scanning confocal microscope (Leica TCS SP5). The fluorescence signal of Graphenin was collected at 505–540 nm. SYTO 17, Hoechst 33258, and MG were used as the positive controls. The fluorescence signal of SYTO 17, Hoechst 33258, and MG was collected at 640–700, 420–480, and 505–540 nm using lasers of 633, 405 and 488 nm, respectively.

MCF-7, Hela, 4T1, Hgc-27, HepG-2, MG-63, Panc-1, HCT-8, A375, A549, and LO2 cells were chosen to evaluate the cytotoxicity of Graphenin. Briefly, these cells were respectively seeded in 96-well plates (5 × 103 cells/well) and allowed to adhere overnight. Then, these cells were treated with Graphenin at varied concentrations (0–24 μg mL−1) for 48 h. Finally, the cytotoxicity of Graphenin was evaluated by the standard MTT assay. For contrast, the cell viability of Hela cells exposed to adriamycin (DOX), cisplatin (DDP), taxol (PTX), camptothecin (CPT-11), and etoposide (VP-16) was separately determined by the MTT assay. The dose of DOX is 0.01, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2 μg mL−1, the dose of cisplatin is 1.5, 3, 6, 12 and 24 μg mL−1, the dose of PTX is 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1 μg mL−1, the dose of CPT-11 is 1.25, 2.5, 5, 10, and 20 μg mL−1, and the dose of VP-16 is 3.75, 7.5, 15, 30, and 60 μg mL−1.

Annexin V-633 and PI double staining (Dojindo, Japan) was utilized to detect the apoptotic and necrotic cells. Briefly, Hela cells were plated in the 6-well plates (1 × 105 cells/well) and allowed to adhere overnight. Then, Hela cells were treated with Graphenin at varied concentrations and incubated for another 24 h. After re-suspended in binding buffer, Hela cells were stained by Annexin V-633 and PI for 15 min. Finally, the stained Hela cells were analyzed by flow cytometer.

The cell cycle was analyzed with the cell cycle detection kit (KegGEN Biosciences, China). Hela cells were plated in 6-well plates (1 × 105 cells/well) and incubated overnight. Then, Hela cells were separately treated with Graphenin and DOX for 24 h. After re-suspended in PBS and fixed with 70% cold ethanol for 4 h, Hela cells were incubated with DNA staining solution (Rnase:PI, 1:9) for 30 min. Finally, the stained Hela cells were analyzed by flow cytometer.

The DNA damage induced by Graphenin was detected by the Comet assay. Briefly, Hela cells were treated with Graphenin for 48 h. The preheated (45 °C) normal-melting agarose (80 μL, 1.0%) was placed on the slide and stored at 4 °C for 10 min. Then, Hela cells were re-suspended with low-melting agarose (75 μL, 0.8%) at 37 °C. The low-melting agarose was added to the bottom layer agarose and stored at 4 °C for 10 min. After lysed in NaCl (2.5 M), Tris (10 mM), and EDTA-2Na (100 mM) at pH 10 for 2 h, electrophoresis was performed in the alkaline buffer at 25 V and 300 mA at 4 °C for 20 min. Following electrophoresis, the microgels were stained with ethidium bromide (EB) and the comet images were captured with a fluorescent microscope. DOX with the same concentration was used as the positive control.

Hela cells were treated with Graphenin and then lysed in cold RIPA buffer for 30 min. After centrifuging, the supernatants were collected and stored. We then utilized a BCA Protein Detection Kit (Beyotime Biotechnology, p0010) to measure the protein concentration. After separated using 12% and 5% SDS polyacrylamide gels, the lysate protein (20 μg) was transferred onto a PVDF membrane using Western blot system (Bio-Rad, USA). Then, the membranes were blocked by BSA and incubated overnight with primary antibodies. The following antibodies were used at a dilution of 1:1000 unless otherwise stated: anti-Caspase3 (CST, 9665), anti-Cl-Caspase-3 (Cell Signaling, 9661T), anti-GAPDH (Abcam, Ab8245,1:500), anti-γ-H2AX (Abcam, Ab243906), anti-Lamin A (Abcam, Ab26300, 1:5000), anti-Caspase-8 (Abcam, Ab25901,1:500), anti-Bax (Abcam, Ab32503), anti-Bcl2 (Abcam, Ab32124), anti-P53 (Abcam, Ab32389), anti-E-cadherin (proteintech, 20874-1-AP), anti-P-gp (Abcam, Ab170904), GAPDH (Abcam, Ab8245). Then, the membranes were incubated with the secondary antibody (HRP-conjugated anti-mouse IgG, Jackson, 115-035-003, 1:5000) for 2 h. The signal was detected using X-ray film for sensitization, development, and fixing in a dark room.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Topoisomerase I and II inhibition assay
ce:section.ce:para: For Topo I inhibition assay, the reaction mixture including 0.25 μg pBR322 DNA, 35 mM Tris, 5 mM DTT, 5 mM MgCl2, 72 mM KCl, 15 mg mL−1 BSA, 1 unit Topo I, and Graphenin was prepared and incubated for 30 min. For Topo II inhibition assay, the reaction mixture including 0.25 μg pBR322 DNA, 1 mM ATP, 10 mM Tris, 50 mM NaCl, 5 mM MgCl2, 50 mM KCl, 0.1 mM EDTA, 15 mg mL−1 BSA, 1 unit Topo IIα, and Graphenin was prepared and incubated for 30 min. After adding SDS, bromophenol blue, and glycerol, the reactions were terminated. Then, the mixtures were subjected to electrophoresis for 30 min. Gels were stained by Gel Red (2 mL) for 30 min and then imaged in a ChemiDoc MP (BIO-RAD, USA).

5-weeks female BALB/c nude mice were obtained from the Slack Experimental Animal Center. All animal experiments were approved by the Animal Care and Experiment Committee of Shanghai Sixth People's Hospital affiliated with Shanghai Jiao Tong University (2020-KY-036(K)). MCF-7 and Hela tumor model was established by subcutaneous injection of 1 × 107 cells in 100 μL serum-free DMEM medium into the axilla of nude mice.

To monitor the drug retention of Graphenin in solid tumors, in vivo imaging was carried out using a IVIS Lumina III (USA). Mice were administrated by intratumor injection with Graphenin (0.75 mg/kg). At 0, 2, 24, 72, and 144 h post-injection, the mice were anaesthetized and imaged by excitation at 460 nm and collection at 580 nm. For contrast, Mice bearing MCF-7 tumors were exposed to DOX (0.75 mg/kg) by intratumor injection and imaging was taken at 0, 0.5, 2, 24, and 36 h post-injection.

Nude mice were randomly divided into 3 groups: PBS, DOX, and Graphenin groups. Mice were received intratumor injection of PBS solution (100 μL, pH 7.4), DOX (1.25 mg/kg), or Graphenin (0.3 or 0.75 mg/kg). Injections were repeated for two times at 7-day intervals. The tumour sizes and body weights were measured every two days. For in vivo long-term toxicity evaluation, major organs and blood were collected for H&E staining, serum biochemistry assay, and complete blood panel analysis.

All data are presented as mean ± standard deviation (n = 5). Statistical differences were analyzed using a Student's t-test. ∗ denotes a statistical significance (∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01 and ∗∗∗p < 0.001) between the experimental data of two groups.

Specifically, we designed amphiphilic, polycationic, N-doped GQDs with a well confined periphery composed of pyridine and phenol moieties for multiple noncovalent interactions with dsDNA (Fig. 1 a). The polycationic GQDs, which could confer unusual antitumor activities as enzyme inhibitors, were referred as ‘Graphenin’ to differentiate it from other functional carbon materials as drug carriers, antibacterial, photothermal, photodynamic, sonodynamic agents [36–39]. In this design, rich pyridinic N atoms at the periphery can be protonated in neutral or acidic solutions to provide a great quantity of positive charge for enhanced ionic bonding with the negatively charged DNA phosphate backbone. Pyridinic N and phenol O at the edge also severed as hydrogen bond donors to form hydrogen bonding with base groups on the floor of the MAG site. The planar aromatic moieties could also enhance hydrophobic interactions with DNA bases, particularly those at DNA breaks. Furthermore, its amphiphilic surface pattern can confer it a good water solubility and overcome the cell membrane barrier [25,33].

Recently, we have developed a bottom-up molecular-fusion strategy to synthesize edge-modified amphiphilic GQDs on a large scale [33,34,39]. Using this strategy, we synthesized Graphenin via the one-step solvothermal treatment of a N-containing aromatic molecule in organic solvents. A high yield (∼60%) of Graphenin was derived from julolidine (C12H15N) in ethanol with addition of a small amount of acetic acid (30:1 v/v) (Fig. 1b). In a control synthesis in the absence of the acid, non-activity GQDs with amino N modification and a high O content (18.7 at.%) were produced (Figs. S1 and S2), revealing that the formation of the bio-active Graphenin formula with the necessary pyridinic N modification and a low oxygen content required acidic catalysis. A proton-catalyzed dehydrogenation and molecular fusion mechanism was proposed for explanation of the production of Graphenin from julolidine.

TEM image revealed that Graphenin has a lateral size from 1.5 to 5.5 nm (3.5 ± 0.5 nm) (Fig. 1d). High-resolution TEM clearly imaged its honeycomb-like graphene structure with sharp edges (Fig. 1e). Its typical thickness determined by AFM is < 1 nm (Fig. 1f), indicating that most Graphenin molecules are monolayer or bilayer GQDs. Its hydrodynamic size in PBS and serum was 8.7 and 5.6 nm, respectively (Fig. 1g). Its Raman spectrum shows a large G/D ratio of 1.33, indicating its high graphitization levels (Fig. S4c).

Graphenin is soluble in many solvents such as water, ethanol, and toluene. Its PBS solutions were transparent without precipitation for two weeks (Fig. S3). Its Zeta potential in aqueous solutions was concentration- and pH-dependent (Fig. 1c, Fig. S5). Notably, Graphenin at pH 7.4 maintained exceptionally high positive Zeta potentials as high as 33.4–72.3 mV at low concentrations (0.01–1.0 mg ml−1), revealing that Graphenin in aqueous solution exists in a polycationic form. The high positive Zeta potential could be one determinant for Graphenin's antitumor activities because of enhanced DNA affinity.

To understand the polycationic nature of Graphenin, its surface components were analyzed by XPS. The XPS analysis reveals its N doping level as high as 10.4 at.% and a rather low oxygen content of 4.7 at.% (Fig. 1h). The N1s spectrum (Fig. 1j) was deconvolved into two main peaks ascribed to dominating pyridinic N (8.4 at.%) at 398.8 eV and graphitic N (2.0 at.%) at 400.8 eV. The O1s XPS spectrum shows one peak ascribed to –OH of phenol (Fig. 1k). The weak –OH signal was also observed in the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1i). The XPS results confirmed that the Graphenin's periphery is majorly composed of a high level of alkaline pyridine moieties, accounting for the polycationic feature. This surface modification was different from that of extensively reported, biologically inactive GQDs, carbon dots, and graphene oxide with negative charge or low positive charge [33,34,36].

As a non-metal quantum dot system, Graphenin can offer superior optical properties for bioimaging [33,35]. Fig. 2 a shows its UV–Vis absorption, photoluminescence (PL), and PL excitation (PLE) spectra in solutions. Graphenin has absorption edge at ∼500 nm, a symmetrical PL peak at 550 nm, a wide PLE spectrum, and high photostability against photobleaching (Fig. S6). It can be excited to emit yellow fluorescence with a quantum yield of 22.6%.

By taking advantage of Graphenin's PL properties, we can directly visualize the Graphenin-dsDNA interactions to determine Graphenin's DNA binding modes. First, Graphenin's PBS solution was titrated with dsDNA to monitor fluorescence change. Fluorescence enhancement and red-shift effects were observed with DNA titration (Fig. 2b), revealing the formation of a Graphenin-DNA complex with enhanced fluorescence compared with free Graphenin. Second, a competitive minor-groove binding assay was performed using Hoechst 33258 as a positive control to determine whether Graphenin could also bind to the minor groove. The 460-nm fluorescence from the DNA-Hoechst complex only showed a slight attenuation in the presence of Graphenin (Fig. 2c), indicating that Graphenin has ignorable minor-groove affinity. Third, a competitive MAG binding assay was further conducted using methyl green (MG) as a representative MAG binder model [21]. Fig. 2d showed the change of the circular dichroism (CD) spectra of ct-DNA + MG with Graphenin titration. For ct-DNA + MG only, MG induced CD signals at 310, 430 and 650 nm. With the addition of Graphenin, the three signals gradually disappeared, indicating that Graphenin excluded MG out of the MAG site. This result supported that Graphenin could be self-targeted to MAG.

Finally, the Graphenin-DNA interactions were further investigated by DNA gel electrophoresis imaging. Small-molecule DNA probes such as MG, Hoechst, PI, and SYTO were used as positive controls. Of them, MG showed the poorest performance in DNA imaging (only the shortest fragment imaged) (Fig. 2e). Interestingly, albeit having the same MAG binding mode as MG, Graphenin exhibited an excellent DNA imaging capability for all DNA fragments (100–2000 bp) in a higher fluorescence contrast than the small-molecule probes. Interestingly, the presence of Graphenin during gel electrophoresis did not retard the migration of all DNA bands, which was different from positively charged CDs [21]. Together, we have identified the binding preference of Graphenin to the MAG and its enhanced MAG affinity compared with that of a typical small-molecule MAG binder. Given positively charged, sharp edges within Graphenin, we proposed an edge-insertion mode accounting for the enhanced dsDNA affinity (Scheme 1).

The cellular uptake and nuclear localization of Graphenin were studied by confocal imaging. Hela cells were incubated with Graphenin and co-stained by the nuclear probe SYTO17 excited at 488 and 633 nm, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2f, the two probes displayed the same nuclear imaging pattern, with fine nucleolus sub-structures distinguishable. It is noted that after a short incubation within 1 min, nuclear imaging of living cells was clearly taken by Graphenin at a low dose (Fig. S7), revealing its ability to rapidly penetrate through intracellular barriers for eventual nuclear localization or targeting. In contrast, small-molecule DNA probes such as SYTO 17 usually require longer-time incubation for bright nuclear imaging (Fig. S8). To further rule out its uptake mode by endocytic pathways, endocytic inhibitors were used to examine the change in Graphenin uptake by Hela cells. Quantitative flow cytometry analysis or confocal imaging show no inhibition of its uptake in the presence of these inhibitors (Fig. S9), further confirming the membrane penetration/diffusion uptake mode for the amphiphilic, polycationic Graphenin molecules (Scheme 1).

After confirming the enhanced MAG binding mode for Graphenin, we further explored its capability for direct interference in the biological functions of typical DNA binding proteins. Topo I and II nuclear enzymes were of special interest as clinically important drug targets for cancer chemotherapy. Topo-mediated DNA relaxation assays were conducted to detect the conversion of pBR322 plasmid to its relaxed form in the presence of Topo inhibitors, including Graphenin and positive controls. For CPT-11 (a typical Topo I inhibitor), the DNA relaxation by Topo I was blocked at a concentration of 35 μg mL−1. In contrast, for Graphenin, a concentration as low as 6 μg mL−1 was required to effectively block the Topo I mediated DNA relaxation (Fig. 3 a). Similar enhanced inhibitory effects were also observed in Topo II mediated DNA relaxation, which was blocked by a typical Topo II inhibitor VP-16 at 60 μg mL−1 and by Graphenin merely at 12 μg mL−1 (Fig. 3c). These results clearly showed that Graphenin could function as a dual inhibitor against both Topo I and II. Moreover, Graphenin can offer enhanced Topo-inhibitory activity compared with classical Topo I and II inhibitors.

It is noted that, only small molecules, typical DNA intercalators, have been found to exhibit Topo inhibition activity. The discovery of MAG binding Graphenin as Topo I and II inhibitors would extend the interfacial inhibition mechanisms [1–6] from small molecules to NPs. Graphenin can be regarded as an exceptionally large plane molecule composed of hundreds of benzene rings, and strongly binds with dsDNA through MAG insertion, leading to the formation of stable DNA-Graphenin complexes on the nanoscale. Based on enhanced Topo enzyme inhibition by Graphenin, we proposed a new mechanism called ‘NP-mediated multitarget interfacial inhibition’. That is, NP insertion into MAG induces greatly enhanced interfacial blockage on the DNA association of a wide range of DNA binding proteins (Topos and beyond) to elicit yet unexplored biomedical effects. Fig. 3b and d demonstrated Graphenin induced interfacial trapping of DNA/Topo-I and DNA/Topo-II complexes. As a result, DNA damage was induced by the Graphenin inhibitor, as evidenced by the Comet assay (Fig. 4 a) and the increased levels of a DSB marker, phosphorylated H2AX (γ-H2AX) (Fig. 4b).

The effects of Graphenin on cell apoptosis were assessed using Annexin V-633/PI staining. A dramatic increase in levels of Hela cells underwent apoptosis (Fig. 4c, Q2+Q3). Graphenin also disrupted cell cycle progression by arresting the G0/G1 and S phases (Fig. 4d). The increased DNA damage level in cancer cells usually induces p53-and caspase-associated apoptosis pathways. To test this, the change in the expression levels of Bax, Bcl-2 and p53 with varied Graphenin concentration was shown in Fig. 4e and Fig. S10. Graphenin treatment increased the expression levels of Bax and p53 (pro-apoptotic proteins), and decreased the expression of Bcl-2, an anti-apoptotic protein. The change in the expression levels of caspase-3 and caspase-8 with varied Graphenin concentration was also shown in Fig. 4f. Without the pan-caspase inhibitor zVAD-FMK, the cleaved Caspase-3 (Cl-Caspase-3) level was increased, but in the presence of zVAD-FMK, the cleavage of caspase-3 was blocked. These results showed that Graphenin induced p53-and caspase-associated apoptosis pathways.

We investigated the effect of Graphenin on the proliferation of cancer cells using the MTT assay. A wide range of cancer lines, including Hela, MCF-7, A549, Hgc-27, 4T1, HepG2, PANC-1, A375, MG-63, and HCT-8, was used to show their wide-spectrum anticancer activities by exposure to Graphenin with varied concentrations for 48 h. Cell viability was dramatically reduced with increasing dose, showing a dose-dependent strong cytotoxicity against all tested cancer types (Fig. 5 a). Half maximal inhibitory concentrations (IC50) were determined to quantitatively assess the anticancer activities against these cancer lines (Fig. 5b). IC50 is around 1.5 μg mL−1 (0.8–2.1 μg mL−1) for different cell types with a small discrepancy. Next, we assessed the cytotoxic levels of graphenin by comparing micromolar-concentration IC50 between graphenin and classical Topo inhibitors DOX, VP-16, CPT-11. Given a size-dependent molecular weight distribution in Graphenin (Fig. S11), an average value was estimated to be around 7 kDa, and thus IC50 was determined to be ∼0.3 μM, comparable to that of DOX (0.36 μM) but much lower than that of VP-16 (12.5 μM) and CPT-11 (4.3 μM).

We further explored the cytotoxic effects of Graphenin on cancer cells with MDR phenotypes. Three cell lines were used as MDR models: DOX resistant MCF-7/ADR, PTX resistant HCT-8/PTX, and DDP resistant Hela/DDP with resistance index of 155.95, 88.41, and 23.31, respectively. Different from classical cytotoxic agents, Graphenin exhibits almost identical sensitivity to sensitive and resistant lines in the three MDR types (Fig. 5c), revealing its potential for overcoming MDR for itself.

To explore the Graphenin's anticancer potential in vivo, MCF-7 and Hela xenografts in BALB/c nude mice were treated for three times by intratumoral (i.t) injection at 7-day interval. The growth of MCF-7 and Hela tumors was markedly inhibited by Graphenin at a low dose of 0.3 mg/kg (Fig. 5d and e). Moreover, at a higher dose of 0.75 mg/kg, all Graphenin-treated MCF-7 tumors were completely regressed (Fig. 5f). In contrast, for the DOX treatment group even at a higher dose of 1.25 mg kg-1, only a limited growth inhibition of MCF-7 and Hela tumors was observed. This result indicates that Graphenin has great potential for cancer chemotherapy.

Using Graphenin and DOX as fluorescent probes, we monitored the retention of Graphenin and DOX locally injected in MCF-7 tumors by in vivo imaging. For DOX, a local high concentration was only maintained within ∼1 h, while for Graphenin, a high concentration was kept for 36 h (Fig. 5g, Fig. S12). This sharp contrast indicates that small-molecule drugs can be rapidly cleared from tumors via rapid diffusion into the remote circulatory system, but Graphenin can be retained for a long time likely owing to its higher Zeta potential and the EPR effect.

The systemic toxicity of NPs is main concerns for their biomedical applications. We initially performed the MTT assay to evaluate the effect of Graphenin on the proliferation of normal cells such as LO2 human liver cells. As shown in Fig. S13, the cell viability of LO2 human liver cells was obviously reduced with increasing the dose of Graphenin, indicating that Graphenin also induces in vitro toxicity to normal cells like cancer cells owing to its non-specific Graphenin-DNA interaction. To further evaluate in vivo toxicity by intravenous injection, 5 mg/kg dose of Graphenin and 2.5 mg/kg dose of DOX were administrated at 2-day intervals. There was no body weight decrease for Graphenin compared with the control (PBS) (Fig. S14). In contrast, marked body weight decrease was observed in the DOX group. The blood analysis also showed no appreciable toxicity induced by Graphenin at the tested dose (Fig. S15). For histology examinations, a slight liver damage and cardiotoxicity can be observed after intravenous injection of Graphenin (Fig. S16), but its in vivo toxicity was much lower than that of intravenously injected DOX. Although Graphenin exhibited relatively low systemic toxicity, it is feasible to develop Graphenin-loaded drug delivery systems such as liposomes, polymer nanoparticles, and micelles that can be passively targeted to solid tumors through the EPR effect because of its ultrasmall size, positive charge, and amphiphilicity. We can also achieve molecular targeting using targeting molecules such as negatively charged aptamers conjugated to Graphenin. Overall, there are many drug delivery strategies that can be considered in the future work to endow Graphenin with targeting ability and reduced systemic toxicity.

In summary, we have developed an environmentally friendly, one-step strategy for controlled synthesis of membrane-penetrating, polycationic GQDs as structurally stable, high-affinity MAG binders. As a photostable DNA fluorescent probe, polycationic GQDs can be applied for DNA gel electrophoresis imaging, nucleus staining of live cells, and nucleic acid detection for diagnosis. More importantly, the MAG-specific binder displayed enhanced interfacial inhibition against DNA binding proteins, such as Topoisomerase I and II, therefore inducing DNA damage and cell apoptosis for a great variety of sensitive and resistant cancer cell lines. Although the potential biological effects of the MAG-targeted binder on cancer metastasis suppression and multidrug resistance reversal still require further studies, several solid tumor xenografts in mice treated by the potent inhibitors showed substantial recession and even complete elimination. The proposed multitarget interfacial inhibition mechanism may further extend to the design of new inhibitors of PARP and cancer-associated transcription factors [3–5].

Bijiang Geng: Investigation, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Writing – original draft. Jinyan Hu: Investigation, Methodology. Ping Li: Visualization, Formal analysis. Dengyu Pan: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Longxiang Shen: Methodology, Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22108168, 11774216, 12175142), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M681267), and the cultivation project (ynms202103) from Shanghai Jiao Tong University affiliated Sixth People's Hospital.
